section_title: INTRODUCTION TO THE ICT ATM METHOD
content:
This teaching will be specifically dealing with the ICT ATM method.
Points of focus in this module:
- Introducing the ICT ATM method
- The ATM in bearish conditions with targets and stop placement
- The ATM in bullish conditions with targets and stop placement

section_title: KEY CHARACTERISTICS OF THE ICT ATM METHOD
content:
The ICT ATM method is a standalone price action pattern. The pattern capitalizes on stop runs. You can
find this pattern on the 60-minute chart. It's relatively easy to spot and you can do it quickly. Finding it is
pretty much a rejection level. It's pretty easy to trade, and it's a complete trading model or setups.

We're going to look at the ATM in bearish conditions. Let the diagram on the right-hand side burn in for a
couple of minutes, and study what it's depicting. When traders first start, the idea of support resistance is
rather early in the introduction to technical analysis. The problem is discerning which support/resistance
levels to use amongst many possibilities. The aim has been to simplify this, making it teachable. This
teaching will be brief but very dense in its information.

Looking at the diagram, think about what would constitute these turning points. You can find similar
patterns across different timeframes. The time frame taught for this pattern is the hourly chart.
Reasons for the hourly chart:
- Gives flexibility to draw down to a lower timeframe to refine risk to a smaller amount.
- Keeps the maximum reward still in sight.
- The hourly chart is clean enough in terms of a time frame.
- Promotes a little bit longer term horizon for the setups.
- You can see a lot of the levels you can see otherwise on a 4-hour or daily if you know what you're looking
for.

section_title: SETTING UP THE BEARISH ATM PATTERN
content:
In your mind, consider how we can use key support resistance levels and what makes these levels key.
First, take your 60-minute chart. This method works on any asset class (Forex, futures contracts,
commodity, stocks, bonds, and the like). The scope is in demo trading only. You start with a 60-minute
chart, pretty simple and straightforward. You don't need to do a whole lot of top-down analysis because the
pattern is self-sufficient.

section_title: IDENTIFYING A KEY HIGH IN BEARISH CONDITIONS
content:
You'll be looking on the 60-minute chart for a key high to form. What makes it a key high is:
- You want to see it create this initial short-term high.
- Then it runs through it.
- Then it breaks down.
- It's going to break a swing low. When price trades through that, that's when it becomes a valid pattern. It
does not become a valid pattern until we get below this swing low here.
Imagine price action creating a 'check mark'. It gives a little sort of short-term high and makes a check like
that. When that check mark gets surpassed by price action, when it trades back up to that, that's the setup.
That's what we're looking for.

- MARKET STRUCTURE BREAK
Ideally, what makes this setup stronger is if this whole price swing is part of a two-stage move. In other
words, we have a short-term high that's ran out, then we have another short-term high here and it runs out.
This move should be ideally the second move up, taking on a short-term high. That means we're pretty
much overbought from a technical standpoint without the necessity of any indicators.
We're focusing again on this short-term low here, and it has to break below that. Our two-stage move
higher, we're going to be waiting for price to retrace back to the swing low that forms prior to the key high
forming. What makes this high key is the fact that we have taken out a short-term high, but then
immediately after that short-term high is violated, it's broken down. In essence, this is a break in market
structure here, and all we're doing is waiting for a retest of that same old support level, which now becomes
resistance. Sell now.

section_title: ENTRY AND CONTEXT FOR BEARISH ATM
content:
When we see this in proper context, we can classify and quantify real support/resistance because we're
incorporating the idea of a stop run above this short-term high. Anyone that's long here will have a stop
below this low, so they run through those stops. Price comes back up to this level here. We've already
rejected price above this short-term high (above number two). So this level here should promote selling,
and it should stave off any real buying because we've broken market structure with this swing low, with this
drop down. So this would be a nice area to look for shorts.

In this entry pattern, we have to frame profit and risk.
- Determine potential profit: We look for a swing low where, in this case, it would be sell stops resting below
that short-term low. We would target from our entry point at this old low down to that level just below the old
low. That's what we are aiming for; that's our target.
- Define risk: The risk is going to be defined by one or two pips above the key high or the rejection high.
Price can go above this short-term low a little bit; it's better if it doesn't, but don't be afraid if it goes above a
little bit. Your stop-loss is up there to do its work. We're looking for the framework of this entry point to this
as our objective and our stop-loss protecting our overall position.

Here we can see price creating a short-term high. Price runs through it. We have another short-term high
here, price runs through that, and then it rejects being above this short-term high and trades down below
this low. Price comes back up and retests, trades to it. This candle here violates it, then we come right back
up to it and trades right into that same level. As soon as that happens, that is a sell scenario or shorting
opportunity. We're looking for a move below this low. Our risk is defined by the high set: 65 pips risk to
make from this entry point down to the stops. That's not bad.

section_title: RISK REFINEMENT WITH OPTIMAL TRADE ENTRY (OTE)
content:
- 15-MINUTE
If we drop down into a 15-minute timeframe, we can take that same insight and zero in, using our trusty
optimal trade entry pattern to reduce some of the risk. Now we can reduce that 65 pip stop-loss down to 20
pips. Notice also that we have a Fibonacci extension of 300 percent which takes us right below that low
where our sell stop target would be.

Now we're going to look at an example of the bullish condition of an ATM. Again, look at the scenario here
in this crude depiction. Again, we're scanning the price action on a 60-minute chart for a key low to form
and a short-term swing high broken to the upside. That's going to be this right here. So we're looking for a
low that's violated and then we trade right back above the short-term high right here. In other words, what
we're looking for is kind of like a crooked little number seven.

- MARKET STRUCTURE BREAK
When that 'crooked little number seven' is violated on the upside, when price comes back down to it, that's
what we're hunting. So ideally, this is going to be part of a continued swing lower. We have a swing low
that's violated here and then we have another swing low that's violated here. So it's like a two-stage move
lower of breaking old support, old support. So now we're really oversold technically without any necessity
of needing any indicators to tell us that.

section_title: ENTRY, PROFIT TARGETS, AND RISK DEFINITION FOR BULLISH ATM
content:
So here's our two-scale drop-down, and we are waiting for price to retrace back to the swing high broken
prior to the key low forming. Again, that's this here, and we zero in right there; that's our setup for a long. So
we'll look for our opportunity for trading our potential reward. Again, we're going to be hunting buy stops
above this swing high here, and the stop-loss is below here. So our entry to our stop is our risk, and our
entry to the buy stops above here is our potential profit or reward.

Here is price action on an hourly chart. You can see we have one support level broken, another area of
support broken, and then we have an old low violated aggressively, and then price trades back above it
right here. When we see that this retest of that old high, that's where we're hunting a long. So if that's our
entry and this is our stop-loss, we're risking 140 pips to make 225 pips (buy stops are our target here). Now
that may not be an ideal scenario for you, and it may not be something that fits your risk appetite.

section_title: RISK REFINEMENT WITH OPTIMAL TRADE ENTRY (OTE)
content:
- 15-MINUTE
So we can now drop down into a 15-minute timeframe and try to get that same 225 pips with a little bit
lower stop-loss. Here we are on a 15-minute timeframe, zoomed in here in that same little area of looking
to be a buyer. We're going to be now removing all of that risk down to 80 pips. So we have a stop-loss just
below this old low here. So we have this low to this high here, coming back down into that level. So we're
trying to give ourselves a little bit more of a better risk to reward model here, right away. We're almost at
three to one, so that's improved.

- 5-MINUTE
Watch what we do when we zoom in a little bit more; we're going to actually down to a five-minute chart
now, and still see if we can get that 225 pips but with a smaller stop-loss. So now we have a 5-minute chart.
Again, that same little area (that green circle) we're zoomed in here, and now I'm doing an optimal trade
entry long, running the Fib from the body's lowest open or close to the highest open or close in this swing
high. It's a beautiful little awesome trade entry long right at the same level we would be looking for that
scenario to unfold, and now we can reduce that stop down to 20 pips but still looking and hunting 225 pips,
which in this case becomes an 11 to 1 reward to risk model.
CONCLUSION:

What we've done is:
- We've looked for a key turning point.
- We've identified the key levels relative to runs on liquidity stops.
- We used the targets in the form of a stop run as well.
- We can use the optimal trade entry to zero in and reduce the risk but still keep the possible potential
reward still the same as we would have used from an hourly setup.
The 225 pips is still available to us with a 20 pips stop-loss. Granted, you've got to hold it for a while, but
this is what it looks like on a 5-minute zoomed out, and you're getting that entire move. It takes a little bit of
time to get there, but nonetheless, this is how we can use the ATM method to get high probability setups
trading key support/resistance levels. Again, to hunt 225 pips is available with a 20 pip stop-loss.
